details.text: Derivative: رَبِّ (Rabb) – The word رَبِّ (Rabb) includes the following implied meanings: المَالِك (Al-Maalik – The Owner), السَّيِّد (As-Sayyid – The One in charge), المُرَبِّي (Al-Murabbi – The Caretaker), المُنْعِم (Al-Mun’im – Giver of gifts and favours) and القَّيِّم (Al-Qayyim – The maintainer). These can be considered secondary meanings or qualities to the primary meaning of رَبِّ (Rabb) which is “Master”. This meaning reflects the essence of our relationship with Allah in the case where He is the رَبِّ (Rabb) and we by necessity are the عِبَاد (‘Ibaad - slaves) which is the plural of عَبْد (‘Abd - slave).

Derivative: الْعَالَمِينَ refers to the nations or worlds of people and Jinn. The use of the sound plural for عَالَم with ياء (Yaa’) and نُون (Nun) indicates that it is referring to the creation that possesses an ‘Aql (mind).

Derivative: الْحَمْدُ – حَمْد (Hamd) in Arabic is a combination of two things. It combines the meanings of praise and gratitude. حَمْد (Hamd) is praise that can only be used in a sincere manner i.e., praise that is sincerely given. Unlike other words for praise like مَدْح (Madh) حَمْد (Hamd) is only used for that which is living and not for inanimate objects. Gratitude is a reaction but حَمْد (Hamd) does not require something to react to. Nouns unlike verbs which have a past, present and future are timeless. Nouns are permanent but verbs are temporary. As such, الْحَمْدُ (Al-Hamd) which is a noun implies that the praise and gratitude to Allah is permanent and encompasses all time i.e., الْحَمْدُ (Al-Hamd – Praise and gratitude) will always belong to Allah in the past, present and future regardless of our existence or any other factor. A verb needs someone to do it whilst a noun does not require a doer. As such, الْحَمْدُ (Al-Hamd) implies that Allah’s praise does not need anyone and is completely independent.

- “The praise and gratitude (الْحَمْدُ) belongs to or is for (لِ) Allah (اَللَّه)” – By stating that the praise belongs to Allah and not one of Allah’s attributes or names you are firstly using the term that no one can have any ambiguity about and secondly you are using the name that encompasses and captures everything about Allah and as such when are saying it you are praising Allah and expressing gratitude to Him for everything that you know and what you don’t know.

Derivative: الرَّحْمَٰنِ (Ar-Rahmaan) – There are three qualities implied in the meaning of Ar-Rahmaan. Firstly, its form رَحْمَٰن (Rahmaan) is upon the فَعْلَان (Fa’laan) pattern form which emphasises the word and increases its magnitude producing the meaning of extremely, incredibly or unimaginably merciful. Secondly, the word رَحْمَٰن (Rahmaan) implies immediacy i.e., that the quality is present right now. Thirdly, the form also implies transience in the sense that it has the potential of being temporary and being taken away, just like the word جَوْعَان (extreme hunger) and غَضْبَان (extreme anger) which are also of the فَعْلَان (Fa’laan) pattern form.

Derivative: الرَّحِيمِ (Ar-Raheem) – There are two qualities implied in the meaning of Ar-Raheem which is upon the فَعِيل (Fa’eel) pattern form. The first quality is that it implies permanence i.e., always, constantly and endlessly merciful. The second quality is that it does not imply that it is necessarily happening right now. This means that although it is permanent it does not mean that it is being applied at every time. For example, someone can be attributed with strength and be described as strong but that does not mean that he is always using or applying that strength at every instant of time.

الرَّحْمَٰنِ الرَّحِيمِ

- Both words الرَّحْمَٰنِ (Ar-Rahmaan) and الرَّحِيمِ (Ar-Raheem) both imply the meaning of mercy as both come from the origin of رَحْمَة (Rahmah) which means mercy. The womb of the mother رَحِم (Rahim) also comes from this word because the foetus is completely taken care of in the womb. The understanding of mercy رَحْمَة (Rahmah) by the Arabs is when all one’s needs are taken care of.
- When two words which are similar are placed together in the Arabic language, then the point is to understand what makes them different and not what makes them similar. The two words combined compliment each other to cover the reality of Allah’s mercy. The potential for being temporary implied in Ar-Rahmaan is removed by the permanence implied in Ar-Raheem. The potential for the mercy not being right now implied in the form of Ar-Raheem is removed by the implication of being right now in Ar-Rahmaan. Ar-Raheem also not does not imply the meaning of being extreme whilst Ar-Rahmaan does. Consequently, الرَّحْمَٰنِ الرَّحِيمِ (Ar-Rahman Ar-Raheem) encapsulates the meanings of permanence, extremeness and being right now. By using both words together the mercy of Allah is encompassed from every angle.
Derivative: مَالِكِ (Maalik) means “Owner” and another recital is مَلِك (Malik) which means “King”.

Derivative: الدِّينِ (Ad-Deen) – دِين (Deen) contains the meanings of being indebted and needing to pay back. يَوْمِ الدِّينِ (Yawm id-Deen) has been understood to refer to the Day of Recompense and Account when justice will be done i.e., the Day of Judgement.
Derivative: نَعْبُدُ is from the word عَبَدَ – يَعْبُدُ – عِبَادَة and combines the meanings of worship and slavery.

Derivative: نَسْتَعِينُ (Nasta’een) comes from اِسْتَعَانَ – يَسْتَعِينُ – اِسْتِعَانَة which comes from the origin of عَوْن (‘Awn) which refers to “help” in matters that you are struggling with and require assistance in. اِسْتِعَانَة (Isti’aanah) means to ask or seek help in something that you are struggling with and need assistance to complete or undertake. The use of this word implies that seeking help only comes after struggle is engaged in and effort has been made.

- إِيَّاكَ نَعْبُدُ (Iyyaaka Na’budu) can be understood to mean: “It is only to you that we give ourselves completely and absolutely in slavery and worship”. It reflects a declaration of being slaves to Allah.

- إِيَّاكَ نَسْتَعِينُ (Iyyaaka Nasta’een) can be understood to mean: “It is only to you that we seek or ask for help”. The fact that the statement “we seek help” is not followed by mentioning what we are seeking help with and has been left open can imply the following: That the one being asked already knows what you need help in. That the list of what we need help in is so long that we just say “help” because we need help in everything. That the help we need is urgent or desperate and as such for brevity we just us for “help”. Finally, as it follows directly from the statement إِيَّاكَ نَعْبُدُ (Iyyaaka Na’budu) “It is only to you that we give ourselves completely and absolutely in slavery worship” it can imply that foremost “It is only to you that we seek or ask for help in order to perfect our worship and slavery to you alone” i.e., being Allah’s slave totally and absolutely requires Allah’s help and assistance.

Derivative: الصِّرَاطَ – صِراط (Siraat) comes from the word صُراط (Suraat) which is a long straight sword. صِراط (Siraat) refers to a path which has no alternative route and has no plural because there can never be more than one صِراط (Siraat). It is also long, wide and associated with danger as it is the only road and more prone to be attacked.

Derivative: الْمُسْتَقِيمَ is commonly translated as “straight”. الْمُسْتَقِيمَ (Al-Mustaqeem) comes from the word اِسْتِقَامَة (Istiqaamah) and means to stand straight implying an upwards movement. The common understanding of the meaning of الْمُسْتَقِيمَ (Mustaqeem) is “straight with no crookedness in it”.

Derivative: أَنْعَمْتَ is from أَنْعَمَ which comes from the meaning of نُعُومَة (Nu’oomah) which means softness, smoothness and tenderness or نَعْمَة (Na’mah) which refers to a life of ease and comfort. أَنْعَمَ (An’amta) therefore implies “You made something easy and smooth”. أَنْعَمَ is commonly understood to mean: “Bestowed favour and blessings (upon)” from نِعْمَة (Ni’mah) which is a blessing and favour.

Derivative: الضَّالِّينَ (Ad-Daalleen) is from ضَلَّ (Dalla) which means to get lost, lose one’s way. It can also imply straying from a path. الضَّالِّينَ is the plural of ضَالٌّ (Daall) which is a noun. The noun in Arabic implies permanence in contrast to a verb which is temporary and tied to a specific time. This implies that the الضَّالِّينَ are a permanent fixture.

- الْمَغْضُوبِ is from غَضَبٌ (Ghadab) which means anger. الْمَغْضُوبِ (Al-Maghdoob) is the recipient of anger. If everyone is angry at you then you are the الْمَغْضُوبِ عَلَيْهِ (Al-Maghdoob ‘Alaihi) i.e., the object of the anger of others. الْمَغْضُوبِ عَلَيْهِمْ (Al-Maghdoobi ‘Alaihim) are those who are the object of anger and its recipients. However, the statement omits stating who is angry with them and could therefore imply more than one source i.e., Allah, the Angels, believers and even people in general. This form also follows a Qur’anic pattern of not attributing or associating Allah’s name with those whom He is angry with i.e., He distances Himself from them which is known as تَبْعِيد (Tab’eed) in Arabic.

- In the Hadith of the Prophet ﷺ he stated that: “الْمَغْضُوبِ عَلَيْهِمْ (Al-Maghdoobi ‘Alaihim) are the Jews and الضَّالِّينَ (Ad-Daalleen) are the Christians”, however the Aayah is general and has also been understood to refer to others who share these attributes.
